subject: concept:epistemic-vigilance
quote: Sperber and some colleagues (including yours truly) called these mechanisms epistemic vigilance, but I will call them here open vigilance, to stress that these mechanisms are at least as much about being open... as being vigilant
rationale: Mercier explicitly names and re-labels the construct; this book is the canonical sustained development.

subject: concept:epistemic-vigilance
quote: human communication is kept (mostly) reliable by a whole suite of cognitive processes—mechanisms of open vigilance—that minimize our exposure to unreliable signals and, by keeping track of who said what, inflict costs on unreliable senders.
rationale: Operational definition: vigilance is a suite of mechanisms with reputational-cost feedback, not a single faculty.

subject: concept:epistemic-vigilance
quote: openness and vigilance evolved hand in hand as human communication became increasingly broad and powerful... If our more recent and sophisticated cognitive machinery is disrupted, we revert to our conservative core
rationale: Mercier's structural claim: vigilance is older default, openness newer layer — inverts the arms-race model.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: the vast majority of mass persuasion efforts, from propaganda to political campaigns, from religious proselytizing to advertising, end in abject failure
rationale: Mass-persuasion-fails is Mercier's central case against the project's strongest breakage claim.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: it is not because the population hold false beliefs that they make misguided or evil decisions, but because the population seek to justify making misguided or evil decisions that they hold false beliefs
rationale: Mercier reverses the causal arrow: false beliefs are post hoc justification, not the upstream cause of bad behavior.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: Trump supporters were provided with accurate information correcting some of Trump's false statements... Most of them accepted the corrections. Yet the supporters didn't waver in their support for Trump.
rationale: Even when receivers accept truth-corrections, behavior doesn't shift — breakage doesn't run through belief at all.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: the most implausible fake news stories... spread largely because they are entertaining... the most absurd political fake news stories might also owe their appeal to their over-the-top nature
rationale: Absurd content spreads not because compressed but because entertaining/bridge-burning — complicates a precondition-count reading.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: the spread of most misconceptions is explained by their intuitively appealing content, rather than by the skills of those who propound them. Vaccine hesitancy surfs on the counterintuitiveness of vaccination
rationale: Memetic fitness as emotion+identity+status is too narrow — Mercier centers intuitive fit with prior beliefs as dominant.

subject: concept:credibility-weighting
quote: a newspaper sends a more reliable signal when it endorses a surprising candidate—one who doesn't belong to the party usually supported by the newspaper—and people are only influenced (if at all) by these surprising endorsements.
rationale: Receivers credibility-weight sources by signal-cost / surprise, not by raw exposure or repetition.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: As a rule, when people are presented with messages from credible sources that challenge their views, they move some of the way toward incorporating this new information into their worldview.
rationale: Direct empirical support: credibility weighting is the rational default; backfire effects are the exception.

subject: concept:conformity
quote: Asch himself never framed his experiments in this way, stressing instead the power of the individual to resist group pressure. After all, the participants followed the crowd only in about one-third of the trials.
rationale: Mercier reinterprets Asch as showing the opposite of common reading — conformity in the strong sense is rare.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: people were perfectly able to follow majority rules rationally: they were more likely to change their minds when a given answer was supported by a larger group, and when it was more consensual (holding group size constant)
rationale: What looks like conformity is rational aggregation (Condorcet) — pressure-tests belief-by-agreement reading.

subject: concept:false-consensus-rational-agents
quote: If ninety-eight members of our imaginary assembly mindlessly follow the ninety-ninth member, the opinion of the assembly is only as good as that of the ninety-ninth member.
rationale: Names the dependence-on-source mechanism by which rational aggregation breaks — same as O'Connor & Weatherall.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: when Helena Miton and I introduced evolutionarily valid cues... participants knew to treat the opinion of these three friends as if it were the opinion of a single friend.
rationale: People DO correct for source-dependence experimentally — partially complicates the pessimistic result while affirming the mechanism.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: Most people who endorsed the rumor—and, according to some polls, millions did—were happy doing nothing about it
rationale: Reframes paradox: rumors are reflective beliefs held without intuitive endorsement, so the share/act gap is by design.

subject: concept:paradox-of-virality
quote: most false rumors are held reflectively, for they would have much more serious consequences if they were held intuitively
rationale: Mercier's mechanism for shared-but-not-endorsed: the reflective/intuitive belief split (Sperber).

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: the effectiveness of [Nazi] propaganda... was heavily dependent on its ability to build on existing consensus, to confirm existing values, to bolster existing prejudices. Whenever propaganda ran against public opinion, it failed abysmally
rationale: Selection-side gates can't override receiver-side priors — selection's autonomous power is bounded.

subject: mechanism:selection
quote: Studies of lying in everyday life suggest that lies are rare—fewer than two a day on average... if the amount of lying wasn't somehow constrained, it would balloon until no one could be trusted anymore.
rationale: Selection by reputation-cost on unreliable senders is the equilibrium mechanism keeping communication stable.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: even people who recognized that some of their views were mistaken... did not change their preferences (voting for Trump). As long as the demand for justifications is present, some will rise
rationale: Demand-side selection: receivers select justifications fitting prior commitments — selection runs through demand.

subject: concept:handle-ability
quote: ideas that don't tap into our intuitive concepts, or that go against them, face severe obstacles from open vigilance mechanisms... we have reasons to reject counterintuitive ideas.
rationale: Counterintuitiveness is the gating variable, not precondition count — supports the project's handle-ability refinement.

subject: concept:manipulation-surface
quote: the most effective messages echo the prejudices or serve the goals of their audiences—anti-Semites defending their hatred with Nazi propaganda, revolting crowds recycling millenarian themes
rationale: The genuine manipulation surface is the justification market, not direct belief-installation — refines what the surface is.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: campaign interventions in the most salient elections (in the United States at least) seem, as a rule, to have no effect... Even if the influence of Cambridge Analytica had been as large as that recorded in experiments on beauty products
rationale: The political manipulation surface is far narrower than the alarm narrative claims — Cambridge-Analytica panic is unsupported.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: the idea that we are locked into echo chambers is even more of a myth than the idea of increased polarization. If anything, social media have boosted exposure to different points of view.
rationale: Captured engagement-gates producing echo-chamber lock-in is empirically dubious — pressure-tests a key mechanism step.

subject: concept:bridge-zone
quote: The Milgram experiment illustrates the dangers of an overreliance on coarse cues to evaluate scientific value. Other examples abound. Pseudoscientists, from creationists to homeopaths, use credentials to their advantage
rationale: Bridge zone is where coarse cues (credentials, mathematization, prestige) substitute for content-evaluation.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: Very few people can properly evaluate scientists' claims... Everybody else is bound to rely on relatively coarse cues. The further removed we are from the group of relevant experts, the coarser the cues.
rationale: Distance from expert community forces reliance on credentials/prestige cues — supports bridge zone as locus of distortion.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: By and large, people on social media mostly look at traditional, middle-of-the-road news outlets; when they are exposed to extreme views, these views tend to come from both sides of the political spectrum.
rationale: Transport-collapse plus selection-capture should converge diets — empirically diets stay broad. Pressure-tests the equilibrium.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: psychology is addressing a number of long-standing problems, as we strive to improve our statistical practices, reduce conflicts of interest... the improved trustworthiness will affect the spread of scientific advances
rationale: Survival of accurate content depends on institution-side criteria that reward truth-correlated signals.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: The wide spread of scientific ideas, in spite of their apparent implausibility, has been underpinned by the solid, even if not unblemished, foundations of trust the scientific enterprise rests on.
rationale: Counterintuitive accurate beliefs travel via institutional trust-foundations, not via raw persuasive force.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: to curb the spread of conspiracy theories... is to have a trustworthy government with strong laws against corruption, conflicts of interests, regulatory capture... conspiracy theories are less prevalent in Norway than in Pakistan
rationale: Trust-dilution loop outcome is set by institutional capture-resistance — directly anchors the claim.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: Mass persuasion fails when it encounters resistance. An audience needs to have positive reasons to believe a message if the message is to have any effect... at best, mass persuasion changes people's minds on issues of little import
rationale: If selection were the steering wheel, tuning would reliably move minds; Mercier shows mass-tuning largely fails.

subject: source:not-born-yesterday
quote: most people have only limited argumentative tools to do so. Those who find the right arguments, such as experts who take the time to engage vaccine doubters in conversation, are more likely to be convincing.
rationale: Integration needs cultivated agents (experts doing argumentative work) plus institutional substrate.
